BSAVA Manual of Guinea Pigs
following exposure (Wright and Dineen, 1972; Hutchison 
and Wright, 1975). Several explanatory mechanisms have 
been described, including a loss of epithelial integrity, a 
reduction or disappearance of cerumen, and a loss of the 
normal Gram-positive bacterial flora. Cerumen has an 
antibacterial action, and so a lack of cerumen contributes 
to the creation of an environment favourable to the devel-
opment of pathogenic Gram-negative bacteria, including 
those belonging to the genera Staphylococcus, Strepto-
coccus and Micrococcus (Wright and Dineen, 1972). 
Prolonged exposure to water may also induce a change in 
the flora from normal Gram-positive bacteria to pathogenic 
Gram-negative bacteria.
Trauma, such as bites or scratches inflicted by cage-
mates, has also been identified as a factor predisposing to 
acute OE in guinea pigs (Boot and Walvoort, 1986). Such 
trauma damages the integrity of the auricular epithelium 
and thus promotes the proliferation of a pathogenic bacte-
rial flora. Certain parasitic infestations (Demodex caviae,

production of free radicals in the middle ear seen in OM 
appears to play a role in the tissue degradation seen in 
this condition (Takoudes and Haddad, 2001).
Clinical signs
The clinical signs of OM are the presence of a serous or 
purulent exudate in the acute phase, sometimes followed 
by osteoproliferation, which can progress to block the lumen, 
or osteolysis in the chronic phase. Dysorexia or anorexia 
with or without associated weight loss, dehydration and 
depression can be present (Boot and Walvoort, 1986; Hawk-
ins and Bishop, 2012). The facial nerve passes through the 
facial canal in the petrous part of the temporal bone, which 
is in close contact with the tympanic bulla. Thus, inflamma-
tion of the tympanic bulla in OM can lead to damage to the 
facial nerve. In these cases, guinea pigs can present with 
facial nerve paralysis and asymmetry of the face (Figure 
21.6) and delayed or absent blinking. If there is also damage 
to the inner ear, corresponding clinical signs may also be 
apparent. In addition to the OM, OI with a peripheral ves-
tibular syndrome can also be present (see ‘Otitis interna’, 
below). Guinea pigs with OM can also show no clinical signs  21.7 Dorsoventral radiograph of a guinea pig skull. The right

BSAVA Manual of Guinea Pigs
(Rossmeisl, 2010). The organization of the semicircular 
canals is such that stimulation of vestibular neurons in a 
bulb generates inhibition of the vestibular neurons of the 
synergistic bulb in the contralateral inner ear. This allows 
an appropriate response of the anti-gravity postural mus-
cles and prevents the occurrence of abnormal posture. In 
the saccule and utricle are other hair cells, each connected 
to an otolith, which together cover the neuroepithelial 
surface of the saccule and utricle. The surface of the sac-
cule is vertical and that of the utricle is horizontal (Ross-
meisl, 2010). These two surfaces make synaptic 
connections with certain dendrites of vestibular neurons 
and generate a continuous nervous tone, which allows 
maintenance of the posture and head position. When these 
surfaces move, for instance when the animal tilts its head, 
this causes movement of the cilia of the hair cells, which 
induces the formation of an action potential and enables 
the maintenance of balance (Rossmeisl, 2010). Thus, in
